meeting.title: City of Alvin Regular City Council Meeting
meeting.location: Council Chambers, City Hall, 216 W. Sealy, Alvin, Texas 77511

councilMember.name: Keko Moore
knownPositions: Expressed ongoing concerns about ETJ release petitions and implications of pending state-level litigation on municipal ETJ authority
Clarified publicly that council is not opposed to ETJ releases per se, but votes against them to formally document objection to the process mandated by state law
At March 19 workshop, inquired whether Cardon PUD would include provisions requiring developer to adhere to specified building standards and lot size percentages
Has stated he is not opposed to smaller lots provided they are designated for commercial or park use, or represent a smaller proportion of residential lots
councilMember.source: April 16, 2026 agenda packet — March 19, 2026 meeting minutes and workshop minutes

executiveSummary.summary: This is a consequential meeting with five items that require real attention. The $2.18M sewer infrastructure contract is straightforward but sets up a year of construction affecting the Highway 35 corridor. The police drone/ALPR grant resolution is low-cost to authorize now but carries a multi-year program commitment and budget ask of up to $275K/year starting FY2027. The commercial vehicle parking ordinance resolves a constituent complaint issue that Council Member DeKeyzer initiated. The ETJ release vote on Ordinance 26-C is another in the continuing series your council has voted against unanimously — same dynamic, different parcel. The ACFR presentation confirms the city's financial health heading into the FY2027 budget cycle.

priorityIssues.agendaItemTitle: Lift Station 33 Rehabilitation and Expansion — Award of Bid to Reddico Construction

detail.whatIsHappening: You're voting on a $2.18M contract with Reddico Construction to rebuild and expand Lift Station 33 along Highway 35, across from Kendall Lakes. This is a single-bidder procurement — Reddico was the only firm that submitted. The station will be expanded to handle 2.65 million gallons per day. Construction starts May 2026 and runs 365 calendar days.
detail.whatDecision: Approve the bid award to Reddico Construction Company, Inc. for up to $2,179,995.83 and authorize the City Manager to sign the contract.
detail.whyItMatters: Lift Station 33 is critical wastewater infrastructure for a corridor that is seeing significant residential growth pressure. The 2015 Utility Master Plan identified this upgrade. Delay risks capacity failures as Preservation Creek and surrounding developments come online. This was already included in the Capital Improvement Plan and is funded by 2020, 2022, and 2023 Water and Sewer Revenue Bonds — no new money needed.
detail.budgetImpact: $2,076,186.50 base contract plus $103,809.33 contingency (5%) = $2,179,995.83 total. Funded entirely by existing bond funds. No general fund impact. Across ~10,500 households, the bond-funded cost represents ~$208/household in previously approved debt.

talkingPoints: This is bond-funded infrastructure the council already approved — voting yes tonight is just executing on the plan. The 2020, 2022, and 2023 bond packages all anticipated projects like this.

Reddico is already under contract for the LS 23 Expansion with no staff performance concerns on record. Single-bidder situations raise price risk, but the engineer of record LJA reviewed the bid and recommends award.

Ask staff tonight: what is the sequencing overlap between LS 23 (currently under construction) and LS 33 starting in May? You want to confirm Reddico has the capacity to run both projects concurrently without delays to either.
priorityIssues.actionItem: Before the vote, ask City Engineer Segovia whether LS 23 construction schedule conflicts with the May 2026 LS 33 start date, given Reddico is running both.

sourceCitations.quote: On March 24, 2026, bids were opened for the Lift Station 33 Rehabilitation and Expansion Project, and Reddico Construction Company, Inc. was the qualified lone bidder. LJA Engineering, the City's consultant that designed the project, reviewed bid that was received and has recommended Reddico Construction Company, Inc. for this project.
This project includes the rehabilitation and expansion of lift station 33 (located along Highway 35 across from Kendall Lakes) to a firm pumping capacity of 2.65 MGD.
Contract Amount: $ 2,076,186.50 (Base Bid, Supplementary, and Allowance Items). 5% Contingency: $ 103,809.33. Total Amount: $ 2,179,995.83
The construction of this project will be funded by the 2020, 2022 and 2023 Water and Sewer Revenue Bonds.
The project is scheduled to start in May 2026 and has a construction time of 365 calendar days.

priorityIssues.agendaItemTitle: Resolution 26-R-20 — MVCPA Grant Authorization: Drone First Responder System and 8 FLOCK ALPR Cameras

detail.whatIsHappening: You're voting to authorize the police department to apply for a state grant that would fund a Drone as First Responder (DFR) program and 8 new automated license plate reader cameras. The grant covers 80% of costs; the city puts up 20%. The annual grant-year city cost is approximately $64,000 for the first year. Without the grant, the same program would cost $150,000 to $310,000 per year. The resolution itself does not commit the city to the full program — it just authorizes the grant application. Chief Lee will administer if awarded.
detail.whatDecision: Authorize submission of a grant application to the Motor Vehicle Crime Prevention Authority (MVCPA). You are not appropriating funds tonight — the FY2027 budget decision comes later.
detail.whyItMatters: APD currently has a 7-minute average response time to Walmart theft calls. The DFR system gets a drone overhead in 90 seconds. The internal memo notes that Preservation Creek — now being built out — is 15 minutes from the station, meaning two officers get tied up on every single call there, reducing effective patrol capacity by one-third or more on some shifts. The grant window closes May 8, 2026.
detail.budgetImpact: Year 1 city match: approximately $64,000 (20% of total program cost). Without the grant, year 1 would cost $150,000–$310,000. The 8 FLOCK ALPR cameras are quoted at $39,200. Full DFR program at 400-ft ceiling is quoted at $275,000/year — the city's share with grant is $55,000. Not currently budgeted; FY2027 appropriation decision comes at budget cycle.

talkingPoints: This resolution just opens the door — the actual funding commitment is a FY2027 budget decision. Authorizing the application tonight costs nothing and preserves the option. The grant deadline is May 8, 2026, so if council waits, the window closes.

The camera deployment is targeted at FM 1462 and SH 288 — the corridor where Preservation Creek and the Pulte/Cardon developments are coming. Council Member Moore, you raised concerns about those developments at the March 19 workshop. This is part of how the department plans to police that growth.

Before the vote, ask Chief Lee to clarify whether the program's multi-year cost trajectory has been shared with Finance Director Gibbs — you want to ensure the FY2027 budget request is already in the queue so the program doesn't get funded by grant in year one and then stall in year two.
priorityIssues.actionItem: Ask Chief Lee tonight whether the $64,000 city match for year one is already flagged for the FY2027 budget submission, and whether there is a written plan for sustaining the program in FY2028 if the grant is not renewed.

sourceCitations.quote: The anticipated city cost for the first year of the grant, for both FLOCK cameras and FLOCK DFR, is approximately $64,000.00. Year two cost would be the same if the grant is renewed. Without the grant, the yearly cost would range between $150,000 — $310,000 based on the DFR program level chosen.

With this grant we intend to add eight (8) FLOCK ALPR cameras to our current inventory. These cameras will be deployed in the area of FM 1462 and SH 288, Alvin West in response to the rapid development of the area.

Over 8 months of Alvin Police Department responding to theft calls at Walmart, the average time from dispatch to officer on scene was just under 7 minutes. A drone that responds to the same area from Alvin Police Department when the call is dispatched can be on scene within 90 seconds.

With the high likelihood that there will be occupied homes in Preservation Creek before the '27 budget year begins, Alvin Police Department will begin responding to a significantly increased number of calls in the Preservation Creek area. Due to the distance from Alvin proper - upwards of 15 minutes in most cases - a typical call response to the area will likely consist of two officers being sent so that backup is nearby should it be needed. This will result in two officers not being available to respond to other Alvin calls for service for significant lengths of time.
Grant Applications from eligible applicants must be completely submitted on-line at https://MVCPA.tamu.edu on or before 5:00 PM, May 8, 2026.

constituentSentiment.note: Databricks/Haystaq credentials unavailable. No modeled constituent data for this item.

priorityIssues.agendaItemTitle: Ordinance 26-D — Commercial Vehicle and Oversized Vehicle Parking Restrictions

detail.whatIsHappening: You're voting on a new parking ordinance that restricts large vehicles and commercial motor vehicles on city streets. The core change: vehicles over 25 feet can no longer park on city streets between 10 PM and 6 AM, and commercial motor vehicles are banned from street parking at all times except for active loading and unloading. A construction zone exemption is included. The penalty is up to $500 per violation per day. This came out of a January 15, 2026 workshop requested by Council Member DeKeyzer after resident complaints about oversized and commercial vehicles parked in neighborhoods.
detail.whatDecision: Approve Ordinance 26-D as written. No budget impact.
detail.whyItMatters: The current ordinance had an unintended consequence: lawn care companies with truck-and-trailer combos exceeding 25 feet were technically in violation for any daytime street parking. The new language fixes that by restricting the 25-foot rule to overnight hours only, while separately banning commercial motor vehicles around the clock (except during active loading). The result is cleaner neighborhoods without penalizing service workers.
detail.budgetImpact: No direct expenditure. Enforcement costs are absorbed by APD. Fine revenue is incidental. No budget amendment required.

talkingPoints: Council Member DeKeyzer requested this workshop in January after constituent complaints. Tonight's vote is the culmination of three months of staff work — redlined draft, final ordinance, and tracked changes are all in the packet. This is a straightforward close.

The ordinance resolves a real practical problem: under the old code, a landscaping crew stopping to work on a house was technically in violation just by parking their trailer on the street during the day. The night-only restriction on the 25-foot rule fixes that without gutting the residential impact goal.
The $500/day penalty structure gives enforcement teeth. Ask Chief Lee tonight whether APD plans a warning period before active enforcement begins, so residents and business owners get notice.
priorityIssues.actionItem: Ask Chief Lee whether there is a planned public notice or warning period before citations are issued under the new ordinance.

sourceCitations.quote: After the council and police department received several complaints about oversize and commercial vehicle parking, Council member DeKeyzer requested a council workshop which was held on Thursday, January 15, 2026.

Article II of Chapter 24 addresses parking (non-commercial) and Section 24-35 which addresses vehicles over 25' in length, has a suggested change to prevent overnight parking, 10p-6a, which will address the issue of lawn companies etc. not being able to park on a city street during the daytime.

No person shall stop, stand or park any commercial motor vehicle on any street, alley or other public way of the city during daytime hours except for the express purpose of immediate loading or unloading. Parking between the hours of 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. is prohibited.

priorityIssues.agendaItemTitle: Ordinance 26-C — ETJ Release, 5.2692 Acres (Moore Property, 14830 Vacek Street)

detail.whatIsHappening: You're voting on another ETJ release petition — this time 5.2692 acres at 14830 Vacek Street in Santa Fe, owned by Christopher and Catherine Moore, who want to build a home there. This parcel sits adjacent to the 4-acre Leigh property that was denied by council on March 19 and then released automatically by state law. The city must act before May 15, 2026, or it releases by operation of law anyway.
detail.whatDecision: Vote on Ordinance 26-C. Council voted unanimously against Ordinances 26-A and 26-B in March — same legal framework applies here.
detail.whyItMatters: At the March 19 meeting, your council voted against every ETJ release petition not because you oppose the individual requests, but to create a formal record of objection to the state-law mechanism that strips cities of planning authority over their ETJ without meaningful local input. That posture is consistent with your stated concerns about pending state-level litigation on ETJ authority. The same logic applies tonight.
detail.budgetImpact: No direct budget impact. Long-term, releasing ETJ land removes it from Alvin's future annexation and service planning area.

talkingPoints: On March 19, the council voted unanimously no on Ordinances 26-A (25 acres) and 26-B (4 acres) — not to block the Leighs from building a home, but to formally document objection to a process that removes local authority. Council Member Moore stated this publicly. Tonight's 5.2692-acre Moore parcel is legally identical.

The adjacent 4-acre Leigh parcel was denied March 19 and then released by state law anyway — which is exactly the dynamic your council objected to. That sequence will likely repeat here if you vote no again. The question is whether the formal record of objection is worth maintaining.

If you intend to vote no again tonight as a protest vote, prepare a brief public statement for the record so the reasoning is documented in the April 16 minutes, the same way Council Member Moore's clarification was documented March 19.
priorityIssues.actionItem: Decide before the meeting whether you want to make a statement for the record tonight consistent with the March 19 precedent, and coordinate with Mayor Pro-Tem DeKeyzer who moved Ordinance 26-B last time.

sourceCitations.quote: On or about March 31, 2026, the City received a Petition for Release from the Extraterritorial Jurisdiction (ETJ) of the City of Alvin from Christopher and Catherine Moore, the owners of approximately 5.2692 acres of land located at 14830 Vacek Street, Santa Fe, Texas.

This property is adjacent to the ETJ release petition that was presented to City Council for consideration at the March 19 meeting, which was denied and subsequently released by operation of state law.
Based on the date the petition was received, the statutory deadline for release, in this case, is May 15, 2026.

Council Member Moore clarified for the public that the Council is not necessarily opposed to the release of properties from the ETJ. However, because state law requires Council to take action on such requests while also providing for their automatic release by statute, he indicated that Council will vote against these items to formally document its objection to the process.

constituentSentiment.note: Databricks/Haystaq credentials unavailable. No modeled constituent data for this item.

priorityIssues.agendaItemTitle: Presentation — Annual Comprehensive Financial Report (ACFR), FY2025 (Crowe LLP)

detail.whatIsHappening: Crowe LLP partner Stephanie Harris will present the city's FY2025 financial audit results. The city received an unmodified opinion — the highest audit rating available — meaning auditors found the financials give a true and fair view of the city's position as of September 30, 2025. Council is being asked to acknowledge receipt. This is a reporting requirement under Texas Local Government Code Section 103.003. The ACFR was filed with the city secretary on March 27, 2026.
detail.whatDecision: Move to acknowledge receipt of the FY2025 ACFR. No substantive vote on financial policy.
detail.whyItMatters: The unmodified opinion matters because the city is in an active bonding cycle — the 2020, 2022, and 2023 Water and Sewer Revenue Bonds are funding projects on tonight's agenda. A clean audit protects the city's bond rating and future borrowing capacity. The March 31 budget workshop showed the FY2027 budget process has already started; the ACFR gives the baseline.
detail.budgetImpact: No direct budget impact from acknowledgment. The ACFR data informs the FY2027 budget process, which City Manager Roland said at the March 31 workshop is underway, with council member budget requests due by end of April.

talkingPoints: The unmodified opinion is significant heading into the FY2027 budget cycle. It confirms the city's financial position is accurately stated, which is important as Council considers additional capital investment for Preservation Creek infrastructure and the growing service area.

City Manager Roland said at the March 31 workshop that council members should submit FY2027 budget requests by end of April — that deadline is imminent. Tonight's ACFR presentation is the right moment to ask Finance Director Gibbs what the actual general fund balance looks like relative to the city's reserve policy.

Ask Stephanie Harris or Finance Director Gibbs one specific question: how did the FY2025 actuals compare to budget projections, and were there any material variances in major revenue or expenditure categories the council should factor into FY2027 planning?
priorityIssues.actionItem: Use the ACFR presentation to ask Finance Director Gibbs for the current general fund reserve balance as a percentage of expenditures, and whether any items flagged in the ACFR management letter require council action.

sourceCitations.quote: For FY25, the City received an unmodified opinion from the auditors – which is the highest form of assurance that our financial statements 'give a true and fair view' of the City's financial position.

Pursuant to Section 103.003 of the Texas Local Government Code, the annual financial statements, including the auditor's opinion must be filed with the City Secretary within 180 days after the last day of the fiscal year (September 30, 2025), and were filed in accordance with this requirement and made available on the City's website on March 27, 2026.
